idea: By next year, a major hospital chain will silently stop using a large vendor's AI radiology tool for triage after internal audit reveals it disproportionately under-prioritizes patients with darker skin tones, and will replace it with a smaller 'explainable' model without publicizing the reason.
context: Non-obvious but cheaply-falsifiable predictions about AI and tech over the next 12 months
why_nonobvious: Most attention is on pre-market fairness testing, not on post-deployment quiet abandonment. Insiders would notice, but outside analysts assume consumer-facing AI gets all the blame; hospital chains avoid PR damage by swapping quietly.
how_to_verify: Monitor vendor earnings calls and hospital chain press releases for mentions of AI radiology tool replacements. Contact 3 major hospital systems (e.g., HCA, Kaiser, Mayo) under pretence of a researcher survey; if none confirm a silent swap of vendor tools accompanied by internal fairness audit findings, the prediction is wrong.

labels.note: Predicting a silent action based on a well-worn AI bias trope is both unoriginal and practically impossible to verify without unethical corporate espionage.

idea: Within 12 months, at least one major US water utility will deploy an AI model to predict pipe leaks using acoustic sensor data, but will not disclose the model's false negative rate until a regulator forces a FOIA request.
context: Non-obvious but cheaply-falsifiable predictions about AI and tech over the next 12 months
why_nonobvious: Smart observers assume AI risk in critical infrastructure is too high for deployment, but utilities face aging pipes and budget pressure; the non-disclosure of error rates is a quiet bureaucratic risk, not a technical one.
how_to_verify: File a FOIA request with a randomly selected large US water utility (e.g., DC Water, LA Department of Water and Power) 12 months from now, and check if they have deployed AI for leak prediction. If no FOIA response includes disclosure of false negatives, the prediction holds; publicly available info showing they haven't deployed or cloud providers confirming no such deployment would falsify it.

labels.note: Checking one utility does not falsify an existential claim, making the proposed test logically broken and prohibitively expensive to resolve.

idea: By next year, a major auto insurer (e.g., Progressive, Allstate) will offer a policy discount for drivers who install a third-party AI dashcam that analyzes road conditions and driver alertness in real time, but will require the cam's data to be shared with a data broker as part of the fine print.
context: Non-obvious but cheaply-falsifiable predictions about AI and tech over the next 12 months
why_nonobvious: Current telematics programs are proprietary and single-purpose; analysts miss the third-party angle. The broker data-sharing twist is hidden in terms of service, not discussed in marketing—most people assume discounts come from insurer-only data.
how_to_verify: Obtain the insurance policy offer documents and terms of service for the discount program 12 months from now. File a complaint with the FTC or state insurance commissioner if data brokerage is not disclosed; if no major insurer launches such a program, the prediction is wrong.

labels.note: Insurers already use telematics and sell data, so predicting they will hide data-broker clauses in dashcam discounts is just predicting the status quo.

idea: Within 12 months, a major online dating app (e.g., Tinder, Bumble) will introduce an AI 'profile trust score' derived from photo metadata and text analysis, but will only show it to paying subscribers, not to free users.
context: Non-obvious but cheaply-falsifiable predictions about AI and tech over the next 12 months
why_nonobvious: Analysts expect AI in dating to focus on match quality or safety, but monetizing trust signals as a subscription perk is a dirty secret—free users would feel stigmatized, so the feature is hidden behind a paywall selectively.
how_to_verify: Buy a premium subscription to Tinder (or Bumble) in 12 months and look for a 'profile trust score' in settings or profile views; have a second free account and note its absence. If no premium subscriber can find it, or if free users also see it, prediction fails.

labels.note: Paywalling a trust score is a predictable monetization tactic, but the proposed test is perfectly cheap, binary, and decisive.

idea: Within 12 months, a prominent mid-range PC manufacturer (e.g., Dell, HP) will ship a laptop with a 'neural processing unit' (NPU) dedicated only to background noise removal in video calls, but will not advertise it as AI—just as 'wind cancelation'—and will disable the NPU for any other use via driver lock.
context: Non-obvious but cheaply-falsifiable predictions about AI and tech over the next 12 months
why_nonobvious: Tech press hypes NPUs for general AI, but a chip locked to a single trivial use is an anti-pattern. Manufacturers want to cheaply claim 'AI PC' without supporting broad workloads, so they stealth-lock the hardware to avoid competitive comparison, which is non-obvious because product reviews rarely test NPU programmability.
how_to_verify: Buy the cheapest Dell or HP laptop with an advertised NPU 12 months from now, attempt to run a standard AI inference benchmark (e.g., ONNX) and verify it fails or uses CPU/GPU. If any other model or lottery reveals the NPU is programmable for other tasks, prediction is false.

labels.note: Artificially crippling and rebranding an NPU contradicts current industry hype, yielding a genuinely surprising prediction with a perfectly cheap hardware test.

idea: Within 12 months, a major social media platform (e.g., Reddit, X/Twitter) will deploy a 'time-to-read' AI that automatically flags and de-prioritizes posts that would take longer than 5 minutes to read, based on estimated reading speed, and will show this metric to users before they click.
context: Non-obvious but cheaply-falsifiable predictions about AI and tech over the next 12 months
why_nonobvious: Platforms optimize for engagement, not reading time; a 'time-to-read' AI that de-prioritizes long content seems counter to growth, but it could reduce cognitive load and improve user experience, which is non-obvious because it requires rethinking the core feed algorithm.
how_to_verify: Monitor the platform's UI or API documentation for any 'time to read' indicator or deprioritization feature; if no such feature appears in 12 months, the prediction is falsified.

labels.note: 'Time to read' is a decades-old Medium feature, making its arrival on X or Reddit a painfully obvious UX copycat move.

idea: By next year, a major AI company (e.g., OpenAI, Anthropic) will release a 'fine-tuning as a service' API that allows users to submit a dataset of fewer than 100 examples to create a custom model, but will cap the output at 500 tokens per response, marketed for very narrow tasks.
context: Non-obvious but cheaply-falsifiable predictions about AI and tech over the next 12 months
why_nonobvious: Current fine-tuning requires thousands of examples and long outputs; a tiny-dataset, short-output service seems too limited, but it could unlock rapid customization for micro-tasks like classification or form filling, which is hard to see until you consider the shift to specialization.
how_to_verify: Monitor the company's API documentation or blog for any announcement of a fine-tuning endpoint with a 500-token output limit and a 'small data' tier; if not released by 12 months, the idea is wrong.

labels.note: Fine-tuning on small datasets is already standard, making a 500-token cap a mundane product tier rather than a novel prediction.

idea: Within 12 months, a major cloud provider (AWS, Azure, or GCP) will launch a 'carbon-aware' scheduling API that shifts non-urgent AI training jobs to times when the regional grid has the highest renewable energy mix, priced at a 10-15% discount to standard compute.
context: Non-obvious but cheaply-falsifiable predictions about AI and tech over the next 12 months
why_nonobvious: Carbon-aware computing is discussed as a long-term goal, but most assume it will be a premium feature or require customer opt-in; a discount-based push to align with renewables is counterintuitive and would shift the economics of AI training.
how_to_verify: Check official cloud pricing pages or press releases for any discounted compute tier explicitly tied to renewable energy availability; if none appear within 12 months, the prediction is falsified.

labels.note: Carbon-aware routing is an obvious ESG play, but checking the pricing page for a discount is trivially easy.

idea: By next year, a popular open-source hardware project (e.g., Raspberry Pi) will release a dedicated 'AI inference hat' that costs under $30 and is specifically marketed for running local LLMs on the Pi 5, but will be limited to 128MB of on-board memory for the model, forcing extreme model quantization.
context: Non-obvious but cheaply-falsifiable predictions about AI and tech over the next 12 months
why_nonobvious: The Raspberry Pi market expects general-purpose AI accelerators; a $30 hat with tiny memory seems too low-performance, but it could enable ultra-local, low-power AI for tinkerers, which is non-obvious because the typical path is to wait for bigger, more expensive hardware.
how_to_verify: Check official Raspberry Pi product listings or distributor inventories for a 'AI inference hat' with 128MB memory and a price below $30; if not announced within 12 months, the idea is wrong.

labels.note: Pi releasing cheap AI hardware is entirely expected, making the hyper-specific 128MB constraint feel like an arbitrary guess.
